original_abstract: Abstract Coordination complexes of precious metals with the d 6 valence electron configuration such as Ru(II), Os(II) and Ir(III) are used for lighting applications, solar energy conversion and photocatalysis. Until now, d 6 complexes made from abundant first-row transition metals with competitive photophysical and photochemical properties have been elusive. While previous research efforts focused mostly on Fe(II), we disclose that isoelectronic Cr(0) gives access to higher photoluminescence quantum yields and excited-state lifetimes when compared with any other first-row d 6 metal complex reported so far. The luminescence behaviour of the metal-to-ligand charge transfer excited states of these Cr(0) complexes is competitive with Os(II) polypyridines. With these Cr(0) complexes, the metal-to-ligand charge transfer states of first-row d 6 metal complexes become exploitable in photoredox catalysis, and benchmark chemical reductions proceed efficiently under low-energy red illumination. Here we demonstrate that appropriate molecular design strategies open up new perspectives for photophysics and photochemistry with abundant first-row d 6 metals.
humanized_abstract: The development of coordination complexes based on abundant, and therefore cheap, metals with attractive functionalities, such as luminescence, solar energy conversion and catalysis, has seen an overwhelming use of second and third-row transition metals with a d 6 configuration like Ru(II), Os(II) and Ir(III). To date, no d 6 complexes based on first-row transition metals affording photophysical and photochemical properties that can compete with the established ones have been reported. A multitude of studies centered on Fe(II) have failed so far to produce emissive d 6 systems with satisfactory photophysical parameters. Here we report that the isoelectronic Cr(0) can give rise to higher photoluminescence quantum yields (up to 15%) and excited state lifetimes compared to any first-row d 6 metal complexes reported to date, while matching that of a well-studied Os(II) polypyridine complex. By virtue of the emitting excited states, Cr(0) complexes show new avenues of applications in photoredox catalysis for which the metal-to-ligand charge transfer states of first-row d 6 complexes are proactively utilized for the first time. Benchmark chemical reductions occur efficiently under low-energy red illumination. Overall our work demonstrates that photophysics and photochemistry of abundant first-row d 6 metals can be opened using suitable design criteria.
